intro:
Coal in the Bible refers primarily to burning embers or live coals rather than the mineral coal of modern usage, which was not employed in the ancient Near East. The Hebrew geḥelet denotes a glowing coal, appearing in contexts ranging from the seraphim's purifying coal on Isaiah's lips (Isaiah 6:6) to the fire of coals on which the risen Jesus prepared breakfast for his disciples (John 21:9). Live coals were the everyday medium for cooking and heating in ancient homes.

The fire of coals carries vivid symbolic weight in Scripture. Proverbs warns against carrying fire in one's lap — a metaphor for approaching sin's destructive power (Proverbs 6:28). Heaping coals of fire on an enemy's head by acts of kindness — cited in Proverbs 25:21–22 and quoted by Paul in Romans 12:20 — may refer to the shame that generous treatment provokes in an adversary, or to coals used ritually to express contrition. The image of God's judgment is repeatedly described in terms of fiery coals: hot burning coals come from his presence in battle (Psalm 18:12–13).
